execution_kernel_notes: Primary path is CodeAgent: the LLM emits Python code blobs as its action at each ReAct step, executed by a Python interpreter. A ToolCallingAgent path is also offered for classical JSON/text tool-call style. Maintainers' own guidance (multi-agent tutorial) recommends ToolCallingAgent for single-timeline tasks like web browsing that don't need parallel tool calls, and CodeAgent for planning/orchestration where 'advanced reasoning will be beneficial'. The ReAct loop is implemented as: `memory=[task]; while llm_should_continue(memory): action=llm_get_next_action(memory); obs=execute_action(action); memory += [action, obs]`. Sandboxed execution is supported via Blaxel, E2B, Modal, Docker, and a WasmExecutor (Pyodide+Deno). LocalPythonExecutor is the in-process default — it AST-walks the code, denies imports not in an allow-list, blocks submodule re-export tricks (e.g. `random._os`), and caps operation count to interrupt infinite loops — but docs explicitly warn 'no local python sandbox can ever be completely secure' and 'The only way to run LLM-generated code with truly robust security isolation is to use remote execution options like E2B or Docker'.

multi_agent_support.notes: Pattern: a child agent is declared with mandatory `name` and `description` attributes; a parent CodeAgent receives it via `managed_agents=[child]` at init time. The parent then invokes the child as if it were a tool inside its emitted Python (e.g. `web_search_agent(task='...')`). State-passing is task-string-in / final-text-out — the child runs its own internal ReAct loop and returns only its final answer, NOT its intermediate memory. This is fundamentally different from AutoGen's group-chat or CrewAI's role-play orchestration: there is no shared transcript, no inter-agent debate, no role assignment by persona — just function composition. Concretely shown in HF docs: a CodeAgent manager with `additional_authorized_imports=['time','numpy','pandas']` calls a ToolCallingAgent web_search subagent that owns WebSearchTool + visit_webpage. The two agents communicate ONLY through the function-call ABI.

tool_protocol: Native Python @tool decorator (typed function -> agent-callable tool); ToolCollection.from_mcp for MCP servers; Tool.from_langchain for LangChain tools; Tool.from_space to wrap a Hugging Face Hub Space as a tool.
memory_model: Per-agent linear memory list of (action, observation) steps appended each ReAct iteration; memory is the prompt-context history that gets re-fed to the LLM. No first-class long-term/vector memory module — users wire one in via tools. Child agents in the managed-agents pattern have their own isolated memory; nothing crosses the parent/child boundary except the task string and the final answer.
supported_model_providers: Hugging Face Inference API / Inference Providers (InferenceClientModel)
observability_story: OpenTelemetry-based tracing; documented integrations with Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, and other OTEL-compatible backends. Step-level traces of code actions and observations are emitted.
typical_use_case: Build a minimal Python-native agent that solves multi-step tasks by writing and executing Python code as its action — e.g. web research with DuckDuckGo + open Python computation, data wrangling, vision/audio pipelines, or web-browsing agents. Ideal when developers want a small, hackable codebase (~1k LOC core) and tight coupling to the Hugging Face ecosystem (Hub, Spaces, Inference Providers).
maturity_signal: growing — 27.5k GitHub stars, v1.25.0 released 2026-05-14, active release cadence; production sandbox integrations (Blaxel/E2B/Modal/Docker/Wasm) shipped; backed by Hugging Face. Still pre-2.0, abstractions intentionally thin, ecosystem younger than LangChain/AutoGen.
one_paragraph_differentiator: smolagents is the canonical modern code-act framework: rather than LangChain's heavy chain/graph abstractions or AutoGen's conversation-of-agents paradigm, every CodeAgent step emits raw Python code that runs in a sandbox, leveraging the CodeAct paper's (Wang et al. 2024, arXiv:2402.01030) finding that code-as-action outperforms JSON tool calls by up to 20% absolute success rate across 17 LLMs on API-Bank. Versus OpenInterpreter — the older code-act baseline aimed at user-facing local code execution — smolagents is library-shaped (embedded in Python apps, not a chat REPL), Hugging Face-native (Hub/Spaces/MCP tool ingest), and self-consciously 'smol' (~1k LOC core) where LangChain is sprawling and OpenInterpreter is application-shaped. Versus AutoGen/CrewAI it eschews role-played multi-agent group chat in favor of hierarchical managed-agents-as-tools — a function-call ABI between agents, not a conversational one.
known_anti_patterns: Docs are upfront that LocalPythonExecutor 'provides best-effort mitigations only and is not a security boundary' — production deployments handling untrusted input without E2B/Modal/Docker/Blaxel/Wasm sandboxing are an anti-pattern. CodeAgent on weak code-capable models degrades sharply (composability advantage disappears). Sandbox-snippet mode (executor_type='e2b'/'modal'/etc.) does NOT support managed-agents because secrets aren't transferred to the remote sandbox, so the child agent's model call would lack credentials — multi-agent + remote-sandbox forces Approach 2 (whole-system-in-sandbox). Marketing-style claim 'core in ~1000 LOC' is genuine but doesn't include the sprawling tool/integration surface.
academic_grounding: Conceptual basis is 'Executable Code Actions Elicit Better LLM Agents' (Wang, Chen, Yuan, Zhang, Li, Peng, Ji — arXiv:2402.01030, 2024). The paper's core claim: code as a unified action space surpasses JSON/text tool-call formats because the latter have 'constrained tool scope and limited composability'. Reports up to 20% higher success rate vs JSON tool-call alternatives across 17 LLMs on API-Bank and a new curated benchmark. Released CodeActInstruct (~7k multi-turn interactions) for instruction tuning; fine-tuned Llama2/Mistral variants improved agent tasks without degrading general capabilities. smolagents docs additionally cite two related code-as-action works: arXiv:2411.01747 and arXiv:2401.00812. smolagents inherits the CodeAgent paradigm itself (code action + Python interpreter + multi-turn revision) but ships it as a production library with pluggable sandboxes rather than the paper's research artifact.
